title: Carbon Brief’s ranking of the most highly cited climate scientists
gist: Carbon Brief's Cosmos 500 author ranking puts France's Philippe Ciais (carbon-cycle modelling) at the top, with the field overwhelmingly skewed to US-based men — almost half of authors are from the US, only 4% from the global south and only 10% women — while emissions-scenario modeller Detlef van Vuuren leads the IPCC-only ranking.

insights.claim: Prof Philippe Ciais of France, whose work centres on modelling the global carbon cycle, tops Carbon Brief's Cosmos 500 author ranking with a citation score of 69,655, ahead of Phil Jones (UEA observational datasets) and Francis Stuart Chapin III.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Identifies the individuals whose work most underpins climate science — the carbon cycle and observational datasets sit at its core.
insights.quote: Topping the Cosmos 500 is French researcher Prof Philippe Ciais, whose work focuses on modelling the global carbon cycle.

insights.claim: Scientists involved in large collaborative projects — such as the Global Carbon Project and core climate models — rank highly because their models and datasets become reference points cited across a wide range of other research.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Explains the structural reason 'infrastructure' scientists dominate citation rankings — useful for interpreting any citation-based influence metric.
insights.quote: physical scientists and economists who are involved in large projects…are likely to rank highly…because their models and data are reference points for a wide range of other climate research.

insights.claim: Almost half of all Cosmos 500 authors are from the US, with the UK (17%) and Germany (7%) next, while only 4% are from global-south countries — two-thirds of those from China.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Quantifies the geographic concentration of authoritative climate science in the global north — an equity and capacity gap.
insights.quote: Almost half of all experts in the Cosmos 500 are from the US.

insights.claim: Only 10% of Cosmos 500 authors are women, and the first 35 entries in the ranking are all men, with the highest-placed woman being the late economist Elinor Ostrom.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Documents a stark gender imbalance among the most-cited climate scientists.
insights.quote: only 10% of the authors in the Cosmos 500 are women. The first 35 entries of the Cosmos 500 ranking are men.

insights.claim: Third-ranked Francis Stuart Chapin III co-authored the 2009 Nature 'planetary boundaries' paper — cited more than 29,000 times per Google Scholar — which defined interlinked thresholds for a 'safe operating space for humanity'.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Planetary boundaries has become a widely used risk-assessment framework — a mental model with reach beyond climate science.
insights.quote: set out the “planetary boundaries” framework for the first time.

insights.claim: A 2005 Science study led by fourth-ranked Gerald Meehl found that even if CO2 had stabilised in 2000, the world was already committed to roughly another half-degree of warming and additional sea-level rise from thermal expansion by 2100.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Establishes the 'committed warming' concept — climate inertia means some impacts are locked in regardless of emissions cuts.
insights.quote: “we are already committed to further global warming of about another half degree

insights.claim: Prof Detlef van Vuuren of Utrecht University and PBL Netherlands is the highest-scoring scientist in the IPCC-only ranking, as lead author of the 2011 'representative concentration pathways' overview that underpinned IPCC AR5 and the new scenario framework for CMIP7 feeding AR7.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Emissions-scenario architects are the most influential figures specifically within IPCC assessments — the scenarios shape every downstream projection.
insights.quote: Prof Detlef P van Vuuren is the highest-scoring scientist in Carbon Brief’s IPCC-only database.

insights.claim: Prof Peter Stott (rank 17 IPCC-only) is lead author of what is widely considered the first study to attribute an extreme-weather event to human-caused climate change — finding human warming at least doubled the likelihood of the deadly 2003 European heatwave.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Marks the origin of event-attribution science — now central to climate liability, insurance and adaptation debates.
insights.quote: the study found that human-caused climate change at least doubled the likelihood of the deadly 2003 European heatwave.

insights.claim: Carbon Brief's citation scores count only references within the Cosmos database (not all-time Google Scholar citations), and author gender was determined via internet searches for pronouns and ChatGPT — an acknowledged source of potential inaccuracy.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Important caveat: the rankings measure within-database influence and carry method-driven uncertainty, especially on demographics.
insights.quote: The gender of authors is based on the usual gender of their first name, as determined by an internet search…and ChatGPT.

source_meta.author: Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
